threats.title: Guardian Proofs Blocked During Proving Pause Prevents Emergency State Correction
threats.hypothesis_description: TaikoL1.proveBlock() is gated by both whenNotPaused and whenProvingNotPaused modifiers. GuardianProver.approve() calls TaikoL1.proveBlock() when the approval threshold is met. This means guardians—the highest-authority tier intended to be the ultimate arbiter—cannot submit corrective proofs when proving is paused. The protocol has no separate guardian-only proving path, creating a deadlock scenario where pausing prevents the very mechanism needed to resolve the issue that caused the pause.
threats.investigation_rationale: Guardians are designed as the emergency backstop for the protocol. Blocking their ability to prove during a proving pause defeats this purpose and could extend protocol stalls indefinitely.

proof_of_signal.pattern: proveBlock has whenProvingNotPaused modifier; no alternative path for guardian-tier proofs

preconditions: Proving is paused via pauseProving(true)
Guardians need to submit a corrective proof for a disputed block
No alternative guardian proving function exists

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: whenProvingNotPaused modifier blocks all proofs including guardian tier
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: No guardian-tier exception in the proving pause logic
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No separate proveBlock path or modifier exception for TIER_GUARDIAN

analysis_questions: Can the owner unpause and guardians prove atomically?
What is the maximum delay between pausing and being able to submit guardian proofs?
Could this deadlock prevent protocol finality indefinitely?
validation_requirements: Confirm GuardianProver.approve calls TaikoL1.proveBlock which checks whenProvingNotPaused
Verify no alternative guardian proving path exists
Assess impact on block verification chain when proving is paused

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: 1) Malicious prover submits invalid proof. 2) chain_pauser pauses proving to prevent further damage. 3) Guardians accumulate approvals but cannot submit the corrective proof because proveBlock reverts with L1_PROVING_PAUSED. 4) Protocol stalls: can't verify new blocks, can't fix invalid proof. 5) Must unpause to fix, but unpausing also allows the malicious prover to act again.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Protocol stall blocks all verification; provers' bonds locked; L2 chain progression halted
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Owner must unpause; but no guarantee malicious actors won't exploit the unpause window

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test for guardian proving during proving-paused state

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Observable via reverted guardian approval transactions

threats.title: Proving Window Indefinitely Extended via Repeated Pause/Unpause by chain_pauser
threats.hypothesis_description: The proving window check in _checkProverPermission uses max(ts.timestamp, lastUnpausedAt) + provingWindow*60 >= block.timestamp. Both pauseProving(false) (via LibProving) and TaikoL1.unpause() reset lastUnpausedAt. A chain_pauser who repeatedly toggles provingPaused (true->false) or the contract pause state resets lastUnpausedAt, extending the assigned prover's exclusive proving window indefinitely and preventing any other prover from submitting proofs for first transitions.
threats.investigation_rationale: If the assigned prover colludes with chain_pauser, they can monopolize the proving window indefinitely, preventing honest provers from submitting alternative proofs and potentially stalling the verification chain.

proof_of_signal.pattern: _checkProverPermission uses max(_ts.timestamp, _state.slotB.lastUnpausedAt) for window calculation; pauseProving(false) resets lastUnpausedAt

preconditions: chain_pauser role is set (resolved via AddressManager)
chain_pauser repeatedly toggles pause state
Assigned prover intentionally does not prove within the original window

initial_code_evidence.suspicious_location: LibProving.sol lines 73-78 (pauseProving resets lastUnpausedAt) and lines 303-316 (_checkProverPermission window calc)
initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: lastUnpausedAt used as lower bound of proving window start; can be repeatedly reset
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: No cap on how many times or how late lastUnpausedAt can be reset relative to block proposal time
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No maximum proving window duration anchored to block proposal time

analysis_questions: How frequently can chain_pauser toggle the pause state?
Does the cooldown window in verification also use lastUnpausedAt, compounding the delay?
Can this be used to permanently prevent verification of specific blocks?
validation_requirements: Confirm that repeated pause/unpause resets lastUnpausedAt each time
Verify that max(ts.timestamp, lastUnpausedAt) produces an ever-extending window
Test that non-assigned provers are permanently blocked while window is extended

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: 1) Block B proposed with assigned prover P at time T. 2) Proving window = T + 60 minutes (SGX tier). 3) P does not prove. 4) At T+59min, chain_pauser calls pauseProving(true) then pauseProving(false), resetting lastUnpausedAt to T+59min. 5) New window: max(T, T+59min) + 60min = T+119min. 6) Repeat before each window expiry. 7) No other prover can ever submit proof for first transition of B.
prerequisites: Collusion between chain_pauser and assigned prover

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Block verification stalled; provers' bonds locked; L2 chain progression halted
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All L2 users and honest provers
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Owner removes chain_pauser role or upgrades contract

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test for proving window extension via repeated pause toggling

technical_investigation_focus.gas_analysis_needed: Two pauseProving calls per extension (~50K gas each)
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Detectable by monitoring ProvingPaused events frequency

threats.title: Verification Bond Payout Uses Stale blk.livenessBond After Top-Tier RETURN_LIVENESS_BOND When ts.prover != assignedProver
threats.hypothesis_description: In the current code, when a top-tier proof triggers RETURN_LIVENESS_BOND, blk.livenessBond is set to 0 and transferred to assignedProver. During subsequent verification in verifyBlocks, bondToReturn = ts.validityBond + blk.livenessBond. If for some reason the top-tier proof ts.prover != blk.assignedProver, bondToReturn -= blk.livenessBond >> 1 (which is 0 >> 1 = 0). However, if RETURN_LIVENESS_BOND was NOT triggered but guardian proves with different prover address, bondToReturn includes the full livenessBond. The concern is about potential future tier configurations where a non-guardian tier has contestBond == 0 (isTopTier) and validityBond > 0, combined with RETURN_LIVENESS_BOND, which could lead to double-payment: livenessBond returned via RETURN_LIVENESS_BOND path + validityBond paid out via verification.
threats.investigation_rationale: While current tier configurations make this safe (guardian tier has validityBond == 0), the code doesn't structurally prevent the scenario. If tier configurations change, the bond accounting could break.
deployment_assumptions: Current tier providers have guardian as only top-tier with validityBond=0
threats.config_evidence: MainnetTierProvider.sol TIER_GUARDIAN: validityBond=0, contestBond=0

proof_of_signal.pattern: RETURN_LIVENESS_BOND path: transfers livenessBond and zeros blk.livenessBond; only gated by isTopTier (contestBond==0), not by validityBond==0

preconditions: A tier configuration exists where contestBond == 0 (isTopTier) AND validityBond > 0
Proof data contains RETURN_LIVENESS_BOND magic value
This is NOT the case with current MainnetTierProvider but could occur after tier provider upgrade

initial_code_evidence.suspicious_location: LibProving.sol lines 176-184 (RETURN_LIVENESS_BOND) and LibVerifying.sol lines 149-158 (bond payout)
initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: RETURN_LIVENESS_BOND guarded by isTopTier only; no check that validityBond == 0
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: If validityBond > 0, both livenessBond (via RETURN) and validityBond (via verification) are paid out
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No assertion that top-tier validityBond == 0 when RETURN_LIVENESS_BOND is used

analysis_questions: Can tier provider be upgraded to introduce a tier with contestBond=0 and validityBond>0?
Would the RETURN_LIVENESS_BOND + verification payout exceed the contract's TKO balance?
Is there a structural guarantee that top-tier always has validityBond=0?
validation_requirements: Check all tier provider implementations for contestBond/validityBond combinations
Simulate full bond flow with hypothetical tier where contestBond=0 and validityBond>0
Verify contract solvency under the combined payout scenario

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: 1) Tier provider upgraded to include a tier with contestBond=0, validityBond=500 TKO. 2) Prover proves at this tier with RETURN_LIVENESS_BOND proof data. 3) blk.livenessBond (250 TKO) transferred to assignedProver, blk.livenessBond=0. 4) During verification, bondToReturn = 500 + 0 = 500 TKO transferred to ts.prover. 5) Total payout: 250 + 500 = 750 TKO, but only 250 (liveness) + 500 (validity) = 750 was deposited. This is actually balanced. The concern is if the validity bond wasn't fully deposited due to reward netting.
prerequisites: Custom tier provider with non-standard bond configuration

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Potential protocol insolvency if bond accounting breaks under non-standard tier config
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All provers and the protocol treasury

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test for RETURN_LIVENESS_BOND with non-zero validityBond top tier

threats.title: SgxVerifier Skips All Proof Verification During Contesting Allowing Unverified Contests
threats.hypothesis_description: SgxVerifier.verifyProof returns immediately without any verification when _ctx.isContesting is true. This means ANY address can contest an SGX-tier proof without actually possessing valid SGX attestation or signature. The contester only needs to post the contestBond (500 TKO on mainnet). While this is architecturally intentional (contests are economic bets), it means the SGX security guarantee does not apply to contests, reducing the bar for griefing attacks.
threats.investigation_rationale: The contestable rollup design allows contests without full proof verification, but this means the cost of contesting a valid SGX proof is only the contestBond, not the cost of obtaining an actual SGX instance. This could incentivize speculative or griefing contests.

proof_of_signal.pattern: if (_ctx.isContesting) return; — skips all verification during contesting

preconditions: Block has been proven at SGX tier
Attacker has contestBond amount of TKO (500 TKO on mainnet)
Contester submits proof at same tier with different transition

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Early return on isContesting bypasses all SGX signature verification
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Anyone with TKO can contest SGX proofs without any SGX capability
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No partial verification during contests; no contest-specific SGX check

analysis_questions: Is the contest bond sufficient to deter speculative contesting?
What is the expected cost/benefit of contesting every SGX proof?
Could systematic contesting delay block verification beyond acceptable thresholds?
validation_requirements: Calculate economic incentive: contestBond loss probability vs verification delay impact
Assess maximum number of blocks that can be simultaneously contested
Verify that higher-tier proof resolution correctly slashes invalid contesters

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: 1) Honest prover proves block at SGX tier with correct transition. 2) Attacker contests with arbitrary (wrong) transition, posting 500 TKO contestBond. 3) SgxVerifier.verifyProof skips verification (isContesting=true). 4) Block verification blocked until higher-tier proof resolves contest. 5) Attacker loses 3/4 of contestBond (375 TKO) when resolved, but delays verification by cooldown period of higher tier. 6) Repeat across many blocks to delay L2 finality.
prerequisites: Tolerance for loss of 375 TKO per invalid contest

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Griefing cost ~375 TKO/block; delays verification by hours per block
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All L2 users waiting for block finality
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Guardian proofs can resolve; attacker eventually runs out of TKO

threats.test_coverage_gap: No economic analysis of systematic contesting cost

threats.title: Unauthorized proving pause: TaikoL1.pauseProving() can be called by anyone (missing access control on pause/unpause)
threats.hypothesis_description: TaikoL1.pauseProving(bool) calls _authorizePause(msg.sender) but does not apply any modifier. However, TaikoL1 overrides _authorizePause(address) with a modifier-only signature and an empty body, meaning the modifier may never execute when called as an internal function. This can make pauseProving callable by any external account, allowing unprivileged attackers to permanently stall proving/verifying (proveBlock/verifyBlocks are gated by whenProvingNotPaused).
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a subtle Solidity pattern: applying modifiers to internal functions only has effect when the function is invoked as a function call; if overridden and called from a base, the compiled code may not execute the overridden version/modifier as intended. If access control is bypassed, a single tx can pause proving and halt the rollup’s finality pipeline.

proof_of_signal.pattern: pauseProving() directly calls _authorizePause(msg.sender); TaikoL1 overrides _authorizePause(address) with `override onlyFromOwnerOrNamed("chain_pauser") { }` (empty body). Potential modifier-not-applied / override dispatch ambiguity.

preconditions: pauseProving(bool) is externally callable (it is).
Overridden _authorizePause() modifier is not effectively enforced at runtime (to be validated by compilation/bytecode inspection).
Proving pause impacts liveness because proveBlock/verifyBlocks require whenProvingNotPaused.

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: access control delegated to an internal call to an overridden function whose body is empty and only has a modifier
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: modifier may not execute as intended; pauseProving becomes permissionless
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No explicit onlyOwner/onlyFromOwnerOrNamed modifier on pauseProving() itself

analysis_questions: Does TaikoL1.pauseProving() enforce onlyFromOwnerOrNamed(chain_pauser) at runtime?
Is _authorizePause resolved statically to EssentialContract._authorizePause (onlyOwner) or dynamically to TaikoL1 override?
What does compiled bytecode for pauseProving() contain (does it inline a resolver check)?
validation_requirements: Compile and inspect TaikoL1 bytecode / run Foundry test: unprivileged caller calls pauseProving(true) and observe success/revert
Check Solidity dispatch rules for internal virtual function override + modifier-only override
If vulnerable: craft PoC showing unprivileged global proving DoS

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Attacker calls TaikoL1.pauseProving(true). If succeeds, all proveBlock/verifyBlocks revert due to whenProvingNotPaused, halting finalization until a privileged party unpauses.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: High systemic impact: chain stalls; bond capital locked; bridging relying on state root sync may halt
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All users/provers/bridges depending on L1 verification
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Requires privileged intervention (if attacker can also unpause repeatedly, can sustain DoS)

threats.test_coverage_gap: No explicit test shown asserting pauseProving reverts for unprivileged callers

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: on-chain events ProvingPaused(bool) emitted; easy to monitor

threats.title: LivenessBond bypass via non-standard ERC20 behavior: LibProposing checks exact balance delta but uses raw IERC20 calls elsewhere
threats.hypothesis_description: LibProposing.proposeBlock enforces tko.balanceOf(this) == tkoBalanceBefore + livenessBond after hooks. However, other code paths use raw IERC20.transfer/transferFrom without SafeERC20 (LibProving, LibVerifying). If TaikoToken is ever upgraded/replaced with a non-standard ERC20 that returns false (instead of reverting) or has transfer hooks that manipulate balances, bond accounting can silently fail (e.g., transfer returns false but function proceeds), breaking bond conservation and potentially causing insolvency or stuck funds.
threats.investigation_rationale: Even if current TaikoToken is standard, upgradeability + AddressManager indirection means protocol correctness depends on token semantics. A single misconfiguration or upgrade can break core accounting without immediate reverts where raw IERC20 is used.
deployment_assumptions: taiko_token resolved address can be upgraded/repointed to a token with non-standard ERC20 return semantics or unusual balance behavior
threats.config_evidence: DeployOnL1.s.sol copies/registers "taiko_token" into rollupAddressManager and ownership is later transferred to timelock/securityCouncil; upgrades/changes are possible via AddressManager.setAddress and UUPS upgrades.

proof_of_signal.pattern: IERC20(tko).transfer(ts.prover, bondToReturn) used without checking return value

preconditions: taiko_token is non-standard (returns false) OR is upgraded to include behavior that makes transfer/transferFrom not revert on failure.
Core protocol continues execution assuming transfer succeeded.
Bond liabilities exist for provers/contesters.

initial_code_evidence.suspicious_location: LibVerifying.sol verifyBlocks token payout; LibProving.sol bond transfers
initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: raw IERC20 transfer/transferFrom without SafeERC20 wrappers or return-value checks
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: silent failure can desync bond accounting from actual balances

analysis_questions: Is TaikoToken guaranteed immutable and standard on all deployments, or can AddressManager point to different token?
Do any code paths rely on transfer returning false to signal failure without revert?
Could a future upgrade of TaikoToken introduce non-standard behavior (e.g., blacklists) that returns false?
validation_requirements: Simulate with a mock ERC20 that returns false on transfer/transferFrom; observe whether protocol continues
Confirm deployment scripts/governance can repoint taiko_token
Add invariant tests for successful token transfer effects matching state changes

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: If taiko_token is misconfigured to a token that returns false, bond payouts may silently fail while state advances (blocks verified), leaving users unable to recover bonds and potentially stalling proving incentives.
prerequisites: Misconfiguration or governance upgrade (not attacker-only on-chain in normal conditions)

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Bonds can become stuck or protocol becomes insolvent relative to expected payouts

threats.test_coverage_gap: No tests shown for ERC20 return-value false handling in proving/verifying paths

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: hard to detect on-chain because transfer false may not emit Transfer events

threats.title: ETH deposit fee deposit can overwrite unprocessed deposits when ring buffer near capacity (fee deposit lacks capacity check)
threats.hypothesis_description: LibDepositing.canDepositEthToL2 enforces pendingDeposits < ringBufferSize-1 for user deposits. However, LibDepositing.processDeposits appends an extra fee deposit by writing to ethDeposits[numEthDeposits % ringBufferSize] and incrementing numEthDeposits without re-checking capacity. Under tight capacity, repeated proposals processing deposits could push the buffer to overwrite an unprocessed entry (or at minimum, lock out new deposits by consuming the last free slot).
threats.investigation_rationale: Even if overwrite is unlikely, capacity edge cases in ring buffers are historically exploitable for griefing/DoS. If overwrite is possible, user ETH accounting on L2 can be corrupted (loss or miscredit).

proof_of_signal.pattern: processDeposits writes fee deposit to ring buffer and increments numEthDeposits without checking (numEthDeposits-nextEthDepositToProcess < size-1) at that moment

preconditions: Deposit ring buffer pending count approaches ethDepositRingBufferSize-1.
processDeposits is invoked (via proposeBlock) when numPending >= ethDepositMinCountPerBlock.
Fee deposit slot index collides with an as-yet-unprocessed deposit due to wraparound.

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: ring buffer append performed without capacity guard
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: fee deposit may overwrite an unprocessed user deposit when buffer is near full
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: explicit require(pending < size-1) before appending fee deposit

analysis_questions: Is it mathematically impossible for the fee deposit append to overwrite unprocessed deposits given the earlier processing in the same call?
What are the exact relationships between numEthDeposits and nextEthDepositToProcess before/after loop and fee append?
Could a proposer intentionally manipulate processing cadence to approach the capacity edge repeatedly?
validation_requirements: Formalize invariant across processDeposits: after consuming k deposits, pending decreases by k but then increases by 1 due to fee deposit; check wrap conditions
Fuzz test with small ringBufferSize to amplify collisions
Simulate adversarial sequence of deposits/proposals to try to trigger overwrite

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Attacker spams deposits to fill the queue near capacity, then triggers proposals that process just enough deposits to append fee deposits at wraparound boundaries, attempting to overwrite a deposit that has not yet been processed.
prerequisites: Sufficient ETH to create many deposits (min 1 ETH each per config)
Ability to cause blocks to be proposed (permissionless unless proposer is restricted)

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Loss or misrouting of L1->L2 ETH deposits; denial of deposit service

threats.test_coverage_gap: No ring-buffer collision fuzz tests for fee-deposit append path are shown

technical_investigation_focus.capital_requirements: high (requires many 1 ETH deposits on current config)
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: requires off-chain monitoring of deposit IDs vs stored slots

threats.title: SGX Instance Self-Replacement Bypasses Key Rotation and Expiry
threats.hypothesis_description: SgxVerifier._replaceInstance does not check if newInstance == oldInstance. An SGX enclave can submit proofs where it replaces itself with the same address, resetting validSince to block.timestamp. This bypasses the 180-day INSTANCE_EXPIRY and the side-channel protection requiring key rotation, allowing a single compromised SGX key to remain valid indefinitely.
threats.investigation_rationale: The INSTANCE_EXPIRY (180 days) and key rotation are critical security mechanisms for SGX-based proving. If an enclave's private key is leaked via side-channel attack, the protocol relies on key rotation to limit exposure. This bug completely negates that protection.

proof_of_signal.pattern: _replaceInstance sets instances[id] = Instance(newInstance, block.timestamp) without checking newInstance != oldInstance

preconditions: SGX instance is registered and valid
SGX enclave controls proof data construction (chooses newInstance address)
No external check prevents newInstance == oldInstance

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: instances[id] = Instance(newInstance, uint64(block.timestamp)) with no equality check
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Enclave sets newInstance to its own address in proof data, signature is valid (self-signed), _isInstanceValid passes
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Compromised SGX key can be used to submit unlimited invalid proofs that pass verification, enabling theft of validity bonds or acceptance of fraudulent state transitions

economic_questions: What is the total value at risk from invalid SGX proofs being accepted?
How many blocks use SGX-tier proofs currently?
What is the cost of a side-channel attack on SGX enclaves?
validation_requirements: Confirm that proof.data can encode newInstance == oldInstance
Verify ECDSA.recover returns oldInstance when oldInstance signs hash including itself as newInstance
Test that _isInstanceValid passes after self-replacement

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: Cost of SGX side-channel attack (hardware + time)
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_execution_cost: Gas for proof submission (~0.01 ETH per block)
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Up to full L2 state manipulation enabling bridge theft

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: Any time after SGX key is compromised
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Could compromise entire L2 state integrity

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: All L2 users and bridged asset holders
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Full loss of bridged assets if invalid state root finalized

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: INSTANCE_EXPIRY=180 days, key rotation per proof by design
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Verify self-replacement path in unit test

threats.title: Guardian Approval Hash Excludes Proof Data Enabling Unauthorized Liveness Bond Return
threats.hypothesis_description: GuardianProver.approve() computes the approval hash as keccak256(abi.encode(_meta, _tran)), excluding _proof entirely. The last guardian to trigger the threshold determines the actual proof.data passed to TaikoL1.proveBlock. A malicious guardian can include RETURN_LIVENESS_BOND (bytes32) in proof.data to trigger liveness bond return to the assigned prover, even though other guardians did not approve this action.
threats.investigation_rationale: RETURN_LIVENESS_BOND causes transfer of livenessBond (250 TKO) to the assigned prover AND sets blk.livenessBond = 0. If the malicious guardian colludes with the assigned prover, this extracts 250 TKO per block that should have been slashed or retained.

proof_of_signal.pattern: hash = keccak256(abi.encode(_meta, _tran)) - _proof is not included in approval hash

preconditions: At least one guardian is malicious or compromised
Malicious guardian is the one who triggers the minGuardians threshold (submits last)
Block's assigned prover would not normally deserve liveness bond return

initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Malicious guardian crafts _proof.data = keccak256('RETURN_LIVENESS_BOND') in their approve() call, other guardians approved different proof.data
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: 250 TKO extracted per block via colluding with assigned prover: 250 TKO * N blocks

economic_questions: How many blocks could be affected?
What is the value of 250 TKO per block extraction?
Could the malicious guardian systematically target blocks where livenessBond should be slashed?
validation_requirements: Confirm proof.data is not part of guardian approval hash
Test that the last guardian's proof.data is what gets forwarded to TaikoL1
Verify RETURN_LIVENESS_BOND path triggers with guardian proof

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: 100% profit (no capital at risk for guardian)

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: Any block where assigned prover failed to prove (should lose livenessBond)

potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: 250 TKO per affected block * total blocks

investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Trace guardian approval flow to confirm proof.data independence

threats.title: Tier Selection Gaming via Predictable On-Chain Randomness
threats.hypothesis_description: Block minTier is derived from keccak256(block.prevrandao, numBlocks, block.number), which is fully deterministic once the L1 block is known. Proposers can compute the required tier off-chain before submitting. On mainnet, 0.1% of blocks require expensive SGX_ZKVM tier. Proposers can simply skip L1 blocks where the randomness would trigger SGX_ZKVM, waiting for a block where only SGX is needed, systematically avoiding the higher security audit.
threats.investigation_rationale: The multi-tier proving system relies on random selection to ensure a percentage of blocks get higher-security proofs. If proposers can game this, the protocol's security guarantee is degraded from '0.1% of blocks verified by ZKVM' to effectively 0%.

proof_of_signal.pattern: meta_.difficulty = keccak256(abi.encodePacked(block.prevrandao, b.numBlocks, block.number)); meta_.minTier = ITierProvider(...).getMinTier(uint256(meta_.difficulty))

preconditions: Proposer has off-chain computation ability (trivial)
Proposer can choose which L1 block to submit in
No penalty for delaying proposals by 1-2 blocks

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: getMinTier: if (_rand % 1000 == 0) return TIER_SGX_ZKVM; else return TIER_SGX
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Proposer computes difficulty for each L1 block, only proposes when difficulty % 1000 != 0
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Proposer saves SGX_ZKVM proving costs (validityBond difference 500-250=250 TKO per avoided block) and avoids more expensive proof generation

economic_questions: What is the cost differential between SGX and SGX_ZKVM proofs?
How many proposers actively game this selection?
What is the security cost of never having ZKVM proofs?
validation_requirements: Simulate tier selection for sequential L1 blocks
Verify proposers can compute difficulty before committing
Analyze historical block proposals for tier selection patterns

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: 250+ TKO saved per avoided ZKVM block

investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Compute difficulty for multiple sequential block proposals

threats.title: Assigned Prover Loses Liveness Bond Despite Proving Correctly When Contested
threats.hypothesis_description: When an assigned prover correctly proves a block but is contested, a higher-tier prover must resolve the dispute. The higher-tier prover becomes ts.prover, permanently locking out the assigned prover (L1_ASSIGNED_PROVER_NOT_ALLOWED). During verification, since ts.prover != assignedProver, the assigned prover loses livenessBond/2 (125 TKO) despite their original proof being correct.
threats.investigation_rationale: This creates a profitable griefing vector: any actor can force the assigned prover to lose 125 TKO by contesting their valid proof for 500 TKO. While the contester loses 375 net TKO, the damage ratio (contester:victim) of 3:1 may be acceptable for targeted attacks against competing provers.

proof_of_signal.pattern: if (ts.prover != blk.assignedProver) { bondToReturn -= blk.livenessBond >> 1; } — penalizes assigned prover even when their original proof was correct

preconditions: Assigned prover has submitted a valid proof at the initial tier
Attacker has contestBond (500 TKO for SGX tier) to contest
A third-party higher-tier prover resolves the dispute confirming original was correct

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: bondToReturn = validityBond + livenessBond; if (ts.prover != assignedProver) bondToReturn -= livenessBond >> 1
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Contest valid proof → higher-tier resolves → ts.prover changes to higher-tier prover → assigned prover loses livenessBond/2
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Attacker spends 500 TKO (loses ~375 net), assigned prover loses 125 TKO. Ratio 3:1 cost-to-damage for targeted griefing.

economic_questions: Is the 3:1 griefing ratio sufficient deterrent?
Can an attacker systematically target specific provers to force them out?
What is the cumulative impact across many contested blocks?

threats.title: Systematic Contestation Griefing to Delay L2 Finality
threats.hypothesis_description: An attacker can systematically contest every valid proof at the lowest tier, forcing resolution at higher tiers. Each contest delays verification by requiring a higher-tier proof + cooldown window. On mainnet: SGX contest (500 TKO) → needs SGX_ZKVM resolution (24h cooldown). SGX_ZKVM contest (1000 TKO) → needs Guardian resolution (1h cooldown). Total cost per block: 1500 TKO. Total delay per block: ~25+ hours.
threats.investigation_rationale: The contestation mechanism allows any party to delay block verification for ~25 hours per block for 1500 TKO. At scale, this can prevent L2 finality indefinitely, blocking bridge withdrawals and cross-chain operations.

preconditions: Attacker has sufficient TKO to post contest bonds (500-1000 TKO per block)

threats.title: Arbitrary ERC20 Theft via Unvalidated coinbase in AssignmentHook
threats.hypothesis_description: The `proposeBlock` function allows the proposer to specify an arbitrary `coinbase` address in `BlockParams`. This `coinbase` address is passed to `AssignmentHook.onBlockProposed`, which uses it as the `from` address in `safeTransferFrom` to pay the `proverFee` in ERC20 tokens. A malicious proposer can set `coinbase` to any address that has granted an ERC20 allowance to `AssignmentHook` (such as other legitimate proposers), and set themselves as the `assignedProver`. This allows the attacker to drain the victim's ERC20 tokens directly to their own address.
threats.investigation_rationale: The `coinbase` parameter is entirely user-controlled and is not included in the `ProverAssignment` hash signed by the prover. This creates a direct authorization bypass where the `AssignmentHook` acts as a confused deputy, spending a victim's approved tokens on behalf of an attacker.

preconditions: A victim has approved AssignmentHook to spend their ERC20 tokens (e.g., to pay for their own block proposals)

threats.title: Assigned Prover Can Evade Full Slashing Penalty via Sybil Proving
threats.hypothesis_description: When an assigned prover fails to submit a proof within their exclusive `provingWindow`, they are permanently locked out from proving that block to ensure they lose their `livenessBond` (250 TKO). However, the assigned prover can simply use a different (sybil) address to submit the proof after the window expires. The protocol rewards third-party provers with 50% of the slashed liveness bond. By acting as their own third-party prover, the assigned prover recovers 50% of their slashed bond, effectively halving the intended economic penalty.
threats.investigation_rationale: The protocol relies on address-based exclusion (`isAssignedPover`) to enforce the liveness penalty. In a permissionless environment, address-based exclusion is trivially bypassed via sybil identities, undermining the economic deterrence against liveness failures.

proof_of_signal.pattern: if (isAssignedPover) revert L1_ASSIGNED_PROVER_NOT_ALLOWED();

threats.title: SGX Instance Address Re-Registration via Missing addressRegistered Update in _replaceInstance
threats.hypothesis_description: When SgxVerifier.verifyProof replaces an SGX instance via _replaceInstance, the newInstance address is NOT added to the addressRegistered mapping. This allows the same address to be re-registered via registerInstance(), creating a duplicate valid instance ID. An attacker with access to a valid SGX attestation can obtain multiple instance IDs for the same enclave address, bypassing the one-instance-per-address invariant and circumventing the INSTANCE_EXPIRY mechanism by rotating between IDs.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a concrete code bug where _addInstances checks addressRegistered but _replaceInstance never sets it for newInstance. Any SGX enclave that has been used for proof verification and rotated keys can re-register the new address, gaining a second valid instance ID.

proof_of_signal.pattern: _replaceInstance sets instances[id] = Instance(newInstance, ...) but never sets addressRegistered[newInstance] = true; _addInstances at line 195 checks addressRegistered but won't find newInstance

preconditions: SGX instance has been used for at least one proof verification (triggering _replaceInstance)
automata_dcap_attestation is registered and functional in AddressManager
Attacker has a valid attestation for the newInstance address from the replaced instance

initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Permanent - the gap exists after every verifyProof call

threats.title: GuardianProver Ownership Not Transferred to Timelock in Deployment Script
threats.hypothesis_description: In the DeployOnL1 script, GuardianProver is initialized with owner=address(0) (defaults to msg.sender), then setGuardians is called, then transferOwnership(timelock) is called. However, since EssentialContract inherits Ownable2StepUpgradeable, transferOwnership only sets pendingOwner — the actual owner remains msg.sender until acceptOwnership is called. The script calls acceptOwnership for sharedAddressManager and rollupAddressManager but NOT for GuardianProver. After deployment, the original deployer retains ownership of GuardianProver and can change guardian sets, upgrade the contract, or pause it.
threats.investigation_rationale: The deployment script uses Ownable2Step pattern but does not complete the 2-step transfer for GuardianProver. This leaves the deployer with full control over the highest-authority proving mechanism.
deployment_assumptions: Deployment script is executed as-is without manual post-deployment ownership acceptance

threats.title: SgxVerifier _replaceInstance Accepts Zero Address as New Instance
threats.hypothesis_description: SgxVerifier._replaceInstance does not validate that newInstance is not address(0). While _addInstances explicitly checks for zero address, _replaceInstance unconditionally writes the new instance. If an SGX enclave's proof data contains newInstance = address(0), the instance slot is effectively corrupted — instances[id].addr becomes address(0), and _isInstanceValid will reject it permanently. This instance ID becomes unusable without admin intervention.
threats.investigation_rationale: The asymmetric validation between _addInstances (checks address(0)) and _replaceInstance (no check) is a code defect. While exploiting this requires a compromised or malfunctioning SGX enclave, it creates a permanent denial of service for the affected instance ID.

threats.title: Residual TKO Token Accumulation in TaikoL1 Without Recovery Mechanism
threats.hypothesis_description: When proofs are overridden or contests are resolved in LibProving._overrideWithHigherProof, only a fraction (1/4 or 1/2) of the loser's bond is paid as reward. The remainder stays in the TaikoL1 contract permanently. Similarly, in LibVerifying.verifyBlocks, when ts.prover != assignedProver, livenessBond/2 is retained. Over time, these retained fractions accumulate as locked TKO in TaikoL1 with no mechanism to withdraw or redistribute them. While this doesn't directly enable theft, it permanently removes TKO from circulation.
threats.investigation_rationale: The bond resolution math consistently retains portions of bonds in the contract. There is no sweep, burn, or redistribution function for these retained tokens. This creates a permanent token sink that grows with protocol activity.

proof_of_signal.pattern: reward = _ts.contestBond >> 2 (only 25% paid as reward); reward = _ts.validityBond >> 2 (only 25%); reward = _ts.validityBond >> 1 (only 50%); remaining stays in contract with no withdrawal path

threats.title: Permissionless proposer can systematically avoid high-cost proving tiers by waiting for favorable per-block randomness
threats.hypothesis_description: Min-tier selection for each proposed block is derived from L1 block attributes (prevrandao, block.number) and the current numBlocks. A proposer can compute the minTier for the next proposal off-chain after the L1 block is known, and simply skip proposing when the sampled tier is expensive (e.g., SGX+ZKVM), thereby reducing effective security sampling and increasing the chance that only cheaper tiers are used in practice.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is an operationally realistic degradation attack: no mempool/MEV assumptions needed. It undermines the intended security budget of random higher-tier audits and may lead to weaker guarantees on blocks that would otherwise require stronger proofs.

High-cost tier occurs with non-trivial frequency (e.g., MainnetTierProvider 0.1% for SGX_ZKVM, TestnetTierProvider 10% for SGX)
No external requirement forces a proposer to propose every L1 block (liveness not enforced on-chain)

threats.title: ETH deposit queue can be permanently blocked by repeatedly forcing near-full ring buffer occupancy
threats.hypothesis_description: ETH deposits are accepted only if pendingDeposits < ringBufferSize-1. An attacker can spam deposits (each at least 1 ETH per config) until the queue approaches capacity, causing subsequent users to be unable to deposit. Because clearing the queue depends on block proposals (processDeposits) and is limited per block (maxCountPerBlock), a sustained attacker can keep the queue near capacity, effectively DoS-ing deposits for extended periods.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a practical, on-chain-only griefing attack. It is costly (requires ETH), but may be economically rational for competitors or adversaries during critical windows. The deposit min amount (1 ETH) increases cost but does not eliminate the possibility, especially if attacker recovers value on L2 or uses the system for their own large transfers while preventing others.

proof_of_signal.pattern: return ... && (numEthDeposits - nextEthDepositToProcess) < ethDepositRingBufferSize - 1;

preconditions: Attacker can afford repeated deposits of >= ethDepositMinAmount (1 ether) up to ring buffer capacity
Block proposals do not consistently outpace attacker deposits enough to drain pending count
No external rate limit / allowlist on deposits

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Pending deposit count stays close to ethDepositRingBufferSize-1; many deposits from a small set of EOAs.

threats.title: Proposer can enforce chosen chain head by abusing parentMetaHash constraint to cause persistent liveness failures for competing proposers
threats.hypothesis_description: The proposer may set parentMetaHash in BlockParams, requiring the parent meta hash to match the current latest stored parent. A dominant proposer can use this mechanism operationally to ensure their blocks build strictly on their chosen chain head, while competing proposers attempting to build on a different perceived head will revert with L1_UNEXPECTED_PARENT. This can reduce proposer diversity and create a practical centralizing DoS at the application layer (not via reordering).
threats.investigation_rationale: Although the check is intended as a safety feature, in a non-public mempool environment with trusted sequencer, competing proposers cannot coordinate via pending tx visibility; parent pinning can be used to repeatedly revert others’ proposals if they rely on stale head information.

proof_of_signal.pattern: if (params.parentMetaHash != 0 && parentMetaHash != params.parentMetaHash) revert L1_UNEXPECTED_PARENT();

preconditions: Multiple independent proposers exist and at least one uses parentMetaHash pinning by default
Competing proposers occasionally operate on slightly stale head state (network delay, RPC lag)
No mempool visibility to coordinate head selection pre-submit

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Frequent L1_UNEXPECTED_PARENT reverts for non-dominant proposers; dominant proposer’s blocks form uninterrupted sequence.
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Ongoing; especially during high latency or RPC instability

threats.title: Any EOA can grief proposers by inducing repeated proposeBlock reverts via invalid blob mode assumptions
threats.hypothesis_description: When proposing with blob DA (txList empty), LibProposing requires blobAllowedForDA=true and blobhash(0)!=0; otherwise it reverts. If proposer infrastructure sometimes submits blob-mode proposals on chains/environments where blobs are not available or blobAllowedForDA is false, an attacker can amplify liveness issues by continuously submitting such failing proposals (or by causing proposer bots to compete with failing strategy), consuming proposal opportunities and operational attention.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a practical operational failure mode: incorrect DA mode selection causes hard reverts. While not a direct on-chain exploit against honest proposers, it can be used by adversarial actors running competing proposers to degrade the network’s block production without relying on ordering attacks.
deployment_assumptions: At least one proposer implementation can be induced (by its own policy) to submit blob-mode proposals while blobAllowedForDA is false or blobs are unavailable on the deployment chain
threats.config_evidence: TaikoL1.getConfig() hardcodes blobAllowedForDA=false, blobReuseEnabled=false (TaikoL1.sol:getConfig).

proof_of_signal.pattern: blobAllowedForDA: false, blobReuseEnabled: false

preconditions: Some proposer bots attempt blob mode when txList is empty
Protocol config remains blobAllowedForDA=false (as hardcoded) or chain lacks blob support

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Repeated L1_BLOB_FOR_DA_DISABLED or L1_BLOB_NOT_FOUND reverts from proposer addresses.
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Continuous until proposer policy corrected or config changed

operational_questions: Do production proposers ever submit txList empty?
Is there a safe fallback from blob mode to calldata mode in clients?
Are there automated alerts for specific revert reasons?
validation_requirements: Inspect proposer client logic for DA selection
Review historical failed proposals and revert reasons

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Run a competing proposer that intentionally submits blob-mode proposals that revert.
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Increase noise/operational load and reduce effective throughput if proposer coordination is weak.

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Reduced block production reliability due to repeated reverted proposals
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Low (client fix), unless config is intended to change

investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: One address repeatedly submitting txList==0 proposals

threats.title: Any proposer can drain TaikoL1's accumulated ETH balance via a malicious hook
threats.hypothesis_description: During `proposeBlock`, `TaikoL1` forwards its entire ETH balance to each hook specified by the proposer (`IHook(hook).onBlockProposed{value: address(this).balance}`). If `TaikoL1` has accumulated any ETH (e.g., via selfdestruct or accidental transfers during reentrant calls), a proposer can specify a malicious hook that simply keeps the ETH. The protocol only verifies that the TKO balance increased by the liveness bond, ignoring the stolen ETH.
threats.investigation_rationale: While `TaikoL1` is not designed to hold ETH (deposits are forwarded to the bridge), any ETH that does end up in the contract is immediately stealable by any permissionless proposer. This violates the principle of least privilege regarding contract balances.

proof_of_signal.pattern: IHook(params.hookCalls[i].hook).onBlockProposed{ value: address(this).balance }(blk, meta_, params.hookCalls[i].data);

preconditions: TaikoL1 contract has a non-zero ETH balance prior to the proposeBlock call

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Sending the entire contract balance to an untrusted, user-specified address
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: The post-hook validation only checks the TKO balance, not the ETH balance
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: Tracking the exact msg.value sent by the proposer and only forwarding that amount to the hooks

analysis_questions: Are there any realistic scenarios where TaikoL1 accumulates ETH?
Does the refund logic at the end of proposeBlock also allow stealing?
validation_requirements: Force-send ETH to TaikoL1 via selfdestruct
Propose a block with a malicious hook that does not return ETH
Verify the hook retains the ETH and the proposal succeeds

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: User accidentally force-sends 10 ETH to TaikoL1. Attacker notices, deploys a malicious hook, and calls proposeBlock with msg.value = 0. TaikoL1 sends the 10 ETH to the hook. The hook transfers 250 TKO to TaikoL1 to satisfy the liveness bond check, and keeps the 10 ETH. Attacker profits 10 ETH minus the TKO cost.
prerequisites: TaikoL1 must have an ETH balance

potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Users who accidentally send ETH to the contract

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing tests verifying contract behavior when TaikoL1 has a pre-existing ETH balance

technical_investigation_focus.capital_requirements: Enough TKO to cover liveness bond
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: On-chain monitoring of TaikoL1 ETH balance and hook transfers

threats.title: Unexpected revert propagation can stall verification if SignalService syncChainData reverts inside verifyBlocks finalization
threats.hypothesis_description: verifyBlocks performs external calls to SignalService.getSyncedChainData and potentially syncChainData after updating verifiedTransitionId for multiple blocks and then updating lastVerifiedBlockId at the end. A revert in SignalService calls will revert the whole verification transaction, preventing verification progress and bond returns, potentially halting the chain if SignalService is buggy/misconfigured or becomes temporarily unavailable.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a realistic liveness risk because SignalService is an external upgradeable dependency. Even if trusted, bugs or config mistakes can cause reverts. The call is in the critical path of block finalization.
deployment_assumptions: Resolved 'signal_service' can revert on getSyncedChainData or syncChainData under some conditions (e.g., authorization, paused state, bad config)
blockSyncThreshold is non-zero so sync is attempted
threats.config_evidence: DeployOnL1.s.sol authorizes taikoL1 on SignalService conditionally; if not authorized or later deauthorized, syncChainData may revert. SignalService ownership is transferred to timelock.

proof_of_signal.pattern: _syncChainData calls ISignalService.getSyncedChainData and syncChainData without try/catch; revert aborts verification

preconditions: signal_service address is set and is callable
SignalService call can revert (auth, paused, internal bug, or misconfiguration)
Verification relies on syncChainData being executed in same transaction (no alternative path)

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: external dependency revert in critical path
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: chain verification progress coupled to cross-chain sync
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: no try/catch or deferred syncing mechanism

analysis_questions: Does SignalService.syncChainData have authorization checks that could fail after deployment changes?
Can _config.blockSyncThreshold be zeroed to disable syncing (it is hard-coded non-zero here: 16)?
Is syncing strictly required for correctness, or can it be made best-effort?
validation_requirements: Review SignalService implementation for revert conditions
Simulate deauthorization/paused SignalService and attempt verifyBlocks
Consider whether a revert would also revert bond transfers and verifiedTransitionId updates (it would), causing liveness halt

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: If signal_service becomes misconfigured or reverts, any call to verifyBlocks reverts, preventing lastVerifiedBlockId from advancing and blocking finalization and bond returns until fixed.
prerequisites: SignalService misconfiguration or bug (or admin action), or dependency outage

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Indirect but high: halts verification and delays/blocks bond returns; may stall rollup finality
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All users relying on finality and provers awaiting payouts
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Admin can fix config/upgrade SignalService; downtime until intervention

threats.test_coverage_gap: No tests ensuring verifyBlocks succeeds when signal_service sync fails or is disabled

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: monitor verifyBlocks reverts correlated with SignalService state

threats.title: Proposer Griefing via Reverting ETH Fee Transfer
threats.hypothesis_description: A malicious prover can DoS block proposers by signing an assignment for ETH payment and then reverting in their fallback function when the `AssignmentHook` attempts to pay them. Because `LibAddress.sendEther` reverts the entire transaction on failure, the proposer's gas is wasted and the block is not proposed.
threats.investigation_rationale: In a competitive MEV environment, provers can collude with specific proposers to censor competitors. By advertising cheap proving services and then reverting when competitors try to use them, they inflict guaranteed gas losses on victims while ensuring only their colluding proposers successfully submit blocks.

proof_of_signal.pattern: if (!success) revert ETH_TRANSFER_FAILED(); propagates revert to proposer

preconditions: Proposer uses ETH for prover fee payment
Assigned prover is a smart contract with a reverting fallback

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: Strict revert on failed ETH transfer to untrusted party
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Malicious fallback function consumes gas and reverts
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Attacker inflicts gas losses on competitors to monopolize block proposals and extract MEV

economic_questions: What is the gas cost of a failed proposeBlock transaction?
Can this be used to reliably censor specific transactions from reaching L2?
validation_requirements: Deploy a reverting prover contract
Attempt to propose a block assigned to this prover
Verify the entire transaction reverts

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Value of monopolized MEV extraction on L2

potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Loss of L1 gas fees per failed attempt
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Cumulative gas costs of honest proposers

investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Check if ExcessivelySafeCall mitigates the revert (it does not, wrapper reverts)

threats.title: ETH Deposit Fee Deposit Re-Processed with Fee Deduction - Systematic Proposer Fee Leakage
threats.hypothesis_description: In LibDepositing.processDeposits(), the accumulated totalFee is written as a new deposit entry in the ETH deposit ring buffer at index numEthDeposits. This fee deposit is treated identically to a user deposit and will be processed by the NEXT block's processDeposits call, where a processing fee is deducted from it. This means the original proposer (fee recipient) receives less than the full fee on L2, with the difference going to the next block's proposer as processing revenue.
threats.investigation_rationale: This creates a systematic ~3-12% fee leakage per block depending on gas conditions. Over the protocol's lifetime, this represents significant misallocation of ETH deposit processing fees between proposers.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Fee deposit written to ethDeposits[numEthDeposits % ringBufferSize] with numEthDeposits++; this entry falls within the processable range for next block, causing recursive fee deduction

preconditions: ETH deposits are being processed (numPending >= ethDepositMinCountPerBlock = 8)
totalFee > 0 (always true when basefee > 0)
Next block also processes deposits

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: Fee deposit at index numEthDeposits becomes a processable deposit for next block; fee = min(ethDepositMaxFee, basefee * ethDepositGas) deducted from it
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Not attacker-controlled but systematically affects all proposers. A strategic proposer could time their blocks to capture other proposers' fee deposits.
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Per block loss: fee ≈ basefee * 21000 ≈ 0.001 ETH at 50 Gwei. If totalFee ≈ 0.01-0.03 ETH, loss is ~3-10%

economic_questions: What is the cumulative fee leakage across all proposed blocks?
Is this an intentional design choice or an accounting bug?
Can a proposer strategically time blocks to capture more fee deposits from other proposers?
validation_requirements: Trace a complete fee deposit lifecycle through two consecutive processDeposits calls
Calculate the exact amount lost per block under various basefee conditions
Verify the L2 credit amount matches expectations

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: ~0.001 ETH per block captured by next proposer
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: 3-12% of fee deposits leaked to wrong recipient

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: High basefee periods maximize absolute fee deduction
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Systematic undercompensation of proposers for deposit processing

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: All block proposers who process ETH deposits
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: ~0.001 ETH per block under normal gas conditions
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Cumulative across all blocks with processed deposits

investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Simulate two consecutive processDeposits: verify fee deposit from block N appears in block N+1 processing

threats.title: SgxVerifier.deleteInstances Leaves addressRegistered Permanently Blocking Re-Registration
threats.hypothesis_description: When deleteInstances removes an SGX instance, it deletes instances[idx] but does NOT clear addressRegistered[instances[idx].addr]. This leaves the flag permanently set to true, preventing the same address from ever being re-registered via either addInstances (onlyOwner) or registerInstance (attestation path). Combined with _replaceInstance not setting addressRegistered for new addresses, this creates asymmetric state: deleted old addresses are permanently blocked, while replacement new addresses bypass the uniqueness check entirely.
threats.investigation_rationale: This creates permanent state pollution that restricts SGX instance management. If a legitimate SGX enclave is deleted (e.g., via rollup_watchdog security response) and later cleared, the same enclave address can never be re-added even after re-attestation, limiting operational flexibility.

proof_of_signal.pattern: delete instances[idx] without clearing addressRegistered[instances[idx].addr]; _addInstances checks if (addressRegistered[_instances[i]]) revert SGX_ALREADY_ATTESTED()

preconditions: Owner or rollup_watchdog calls deleteInstances
The deleted instance address needs to be re-registered later

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: addressRegistered[addr] remains true after delete instances[idx]
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: State asymmetry: _replaceInstance doesn't set addressRegistered for newInstance, allowing new addresses to bypass uniqueness; deleteInstances doesn't clear it, blocking re-registration
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Operational cost: forces creation of new enclave keys instead of re-using attested ones; potential protocol degradation if SGX instances become scarce

economic_questions: How many SGX instances could be permanently orphaned over the protocol's lifetime?
What is the cost of deploying new SGX enclave keys vs re-using existing ones?
Can an attacker strategically trigger deleteInstances to reduce the available SGX prover pool?
validation_requirements: Confirm deleteInstances + addInstances with same address reverts
Confirm _replaceInstance newInstance address can bypass addressRegistered check
Test operational recovery scenarios after instance deletion

market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Reduced SGX prover pool availability over time

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: SGX prover operators who need to re-register after deletion
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Operational cost of creating new enclave keys
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Proportional to number of deleted instances over protocol lifetime

investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Test deleteInstances -> registerInstance with same address

threats.title: LibProving._createTransition Unchecked nextTransitionId Overflow Corrupts Transition State
threats.hypothesis_description: In _createTransition, nextTransitionId is incremented in an unchecked block: `tid_ = _blk.nextTransitionId++`. Being a uint32, after 2^32 increments it overflows to 0. Transition ID 0 is the uninitialized state, and overflowing would cause new transitions to map to ID 0, colliding with the reserved sentinel value. While the comment states this is unrealistic, each transition requires a unique parentHash and a separate proveBlock call, making griefing expensive but theoretically possible over extended time with low-cost tier proofs.
threats.investigation_rationale: Even if not immediately exploitable, this is a code correctness defect. If the overflow occurs, the getTransitionId check `tid_ >= _blk.nextTransitionId` would behave incorrectly (nextTransitionId = 0 after overflow), potentially allowing all transition IDs to appear valid.

proof_of_signal.pattern: unchecked { tid_ = _blk.nextTransitionId++; } with uint32 type allowing wraparound to 0

preconditions: Attacker can submit 2^32 unique parentHash values for a single block
Each submission requires a valid proof at appropriate tier
Sufficient TKO for validity bonds (though bonds may be partially recovered)

initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: After overflow, nextTransitionId = 0, causing getTransitionId check `tid_ >= _blk.nextTransitionId` to always be true (any tid >= 0), making all stored transitions appear valid
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Post-overflow: attacker could create a transition that collides with existing verified data, potentially corrupting block verification chain

economic_questions: What is the minimum cost to create 2^32 transitions (considering gas + bonds)?
What is the behavior of getTransitionId when nextTransitionId overflows to 0?
Could this be triggered on a devnet or testnet with cheaper tier configurations?
validation_requirements: Test uint32 overflow behavior in _createTransition
Verify getTransitionId behavior when nextTransitionId = 0
Calculate total cost of 2^32 proveBlock calls

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: Astronomical (billions in gas + bonds) for mainnet, potentially feasible on devnet
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: Negative on mainnet; potentially positive on devnet/testnet
preliminary_attack_economics.attack_duration: Years on mainnet; potentially achievable on low-cost chains

market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Total protocol failure if overflow achieved

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: uint32 max = 4,294,967,296; tier bond costs per transition
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Verify overflow behavior and downstream impact on getTransitionId

threats.title: SGX Instance Duplication via Missing Registration State Update
threats.hypothesis_description: When an SGX instance rotates its key, the new instance address is not marked as registered. This allows the enclave to re-register the same new address via the attestation flow, obtaining multiple valid instance IDs for a single key.
threats.investigation_rationale: The _replaceInstance function fails to set addressRegistered[newInstance] = true. An attacker can use the Automata DCAP attestation for the new key to call registerInstance(), which will succeed and create a duplicate instance ID. This breaks the 1-to-1 mapping invariant and allows the enclave to juggle multiple IDs to bypass operational limits.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Missing addressRegistered[newInstance] = true; in _replaceInstance

preconditions: Attacker controls a valid SGX enclave
Attacker can generate a valid DCAP attestation for the new key

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: State variable tracking uniqueness is not updated during the lifecycle of the object

operational_questions: Can an enclave generate an attestation quote for a key it just rotated to?
Does the protocol rely on instance ID uniqueness for any off-chain monitoring or slashing?
validation_requirements: Rotate an SGX key via verifyProof
Call registerInstance with an attestation for the new key
Verify a second instance ID is created for the same address

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Submit a proof that rotates the SGX key from oldInstance to newInstance
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Generate a DCAP attestation quote for newInstance
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: SgxVerifier creates a new instance ID for newInstance, giving the enclave two active IDs
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Requires custom SGX client to extract quotes

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Corruption of the SGX registry, potential bypass of instance-level rate limits or slashing
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Requires contract upgrade to fix state tracking

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Multiple instance IDs mapping to the same address
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Rapid registration of instances immediately following a rotation

threats.title: TaikoTimelockController getMinDelay Override Allows Instant Execution for TIMELOCK_ADMIN_ROLE
threats.hypothesis_description: TaikoTimelockController.getMinDelay() returns 0 for callers with TIMELOCK_ADMIN_ROLE, completely bypassing the 7-day timelock delay. Deployment scripts grant TIMELOCK_ADMIN_ROLE to the securityCouncil. If this role is compromised, an attacker can schedule and execute governance operations (contract upgrades, address manager changes, fund extraction) in the same block with zero delay.
threats.investigation_rationale: The timelock's purpose is to give users time to react to governance changes. Returning 0 delay for admin negates this protection. The deployment grants this role to securityCouncil, making it a high-value target. The UpgradeScript.s.sol shows that upgrades go through timelock.schedule -> timelock.execute, and with delay=0 these execute atomically.
threats.config_evidence: DeployOnL1.s.sol line: _timelock.grantRole(_timelock.TIMELOCK_ADMIN_ROLE(), securityCouncil); confirms securityCouncil gets admin role

proof_of_signal.pattern: return hasRole(TIMELOCK_ADMIN_ROLE, msg.sender) ? 0 : super.getMinDelay();

preconditions: Attacker obtains TIMELOCK_ADMIN_ROLE (compromise of securityCouncil)
Attacker also has PROPOSER_ROLE and EXECUTOR_ROLE (securityCouncil has all three per deployment)

initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Complete timelock bypass; all governance protections nullified
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No minimum delay floor; no multi-step approval for admin operations

analysis_questions: What operations can be executed via timelock that would cause maximum damage?
Is there any on-chain mechanism to detect or prevent instant execution?
Can the admin role itself be transferred or revoked through the zero-delay path?
validation_requirements: Confirm that schedule + execute with delay=0 completes in same transaction
Trace all contracts whose ownership is the timelock to assess blast radius
Verify deployment script role assignments

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: 1) SecurityCouncil key(s) compromised. 2) Attacker calls timelock.schedule with malicious payload (e.g., AddressManager.setAddress to replace bridge with attacker contract) with minDelay=0. 3) Attacker calls timelock.execute immediately. 4) Bridge contract replaced; attacker drains bridged assets.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Total protocol takeover; all bridged assets, tokens, and contract logic at risk
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: None once malicious operations are executed

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test verifying timelock delay cannot be bypassed

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Detectable via timelock events but too late to prevent

threats.title: Proving Window Indefinite Extension via Pause/Unpause Toggle by chain_pauser
threats.hypothesis_description: The proving window check in _checkProverPermission uses max(ts.timestamp, lastUnpausedAt) as the window start time. Both pauseProving(false) and TaikoL1.unpause() reset lastUnpausedAt to current timestamp. A malicious chain_pauser can repeatedly toggle pauseProving(true)/pauseProving(false), each time resetting lastUnpausedAt. This keeps the assigned prover's exclusive window perpetually open (for tid==1, tier==0), preventing any other prover from submitting proofs for first transitions, stalling block verification indefinitely.
threats.investigation_rationale: The lastUnpausedAt variable is reset on every unpause event. Combined with the proving window check that uses max(ts.timestamp, lastUnpausedAt), repeated toggling prevents the proving window from ever expiring.

proof_of_signal.pattern: bool inProvingWindow = uint256(_ts.timestamp).max(_state.slotB.lastUnpausedAt) + _tier.provingWindow * 60 >= block.timestamp; — lastUnpausedAt is reset on each unpause, extending the window

preconditions: chain_pauser role is compromised or colluding with assigned prover
chain_pauser can call pauseProving(true) and then pauseProving(false) repeatedly
Assigned prover intentionally does not prove, keeping tid==1, tier==0

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: pauseProving(false) resets lastUnpausedAt; _checkProverPermission uses lastUnpausedAt as window start; repeated toggles keep window open

operational_questions: Is chain_pauser separate from owner? Who controls it?
How often can pauseProving be toggled without detection?
What monitoring exists for proving pause events?
validation_requirements: Confirm lastUnpausedAt is reset on each pauseProving(false)
Test that repeated toggle keeps inProvingWindow true indefinitely
Verify that no other prover can override during the forced window

threats.title: TaikoTimelockController Admin Role Bypasses Minimum Delay for Instant Governance Execution
threats.hypothesis_description: TaikoTimelockController.getMinDelay() returns 0 for any caller with TIMELOCK_ADMIN_ROLE, completely bypassing the 7-day minimum delay. The deployment script grants TIMELOCK_ADMIN_ROLE to securityCouncil. If the securityCouncil key is compromised, an attacker can schedule AND execute operations in the same block, replacing critical contract addresses in AddressManager (bridge, signal_service, verifiers) with malicious contracts, draining bridged assets or corrupting L2 state.
threats.investigation_rationale: The code explicitly returns 0 delay for admin role holders. threats.title: Malicious chain_pauser can indefinitely extend the assigned prover's exclusive window by toggling pause
threats.hypothesis_description: In `LibProving`, the proving window expiration is calculated as `max(ts.timestamp, lastUnpausedAt) + provingWindow * 60`. threats.title: LibUtils.getTransitionId Stale Ring Buffer Key Causes Verification Revert Instead of Graceful Fallthrough
threats.hypothesis_description: In LibUtils.getTransitionId, when a ring buffer slot is reused by a new block, the stale transitions[_slot][1].key from the previous block remains in storage. If the current block's parentHash coincidentally matches this stale key, getTransitionId returns tid=1. But for a newly proposed block (nextTransitionId=1), the check `tid_ >= _blk.nextTransitionId` evaluates to `1 >= 1`, causing a revert with L1_UNEXPECTED_TRANSITION_ID instead of returning 0. In LibVerifying.verifyBlocks, this revert propagates, halting the entire verification loop instead of gracefully breaking to try later.
threats.investigation_rationale: While a parentHash collision with a stale key is astronomically unlikely (1/2^256), the code's failure mode is incorrect: it should return 0 (not found) rather than revert, to maintain graceful verification progression. This represents a code correctness issue in edge case handling.

proof_of_signal.pattern: if (_state.transitions[_slot][1].key == _parentHash) tid_ = 1; followed by if (tid_ >= _blk.nextTransitionId) revert; for new block with nextTransitionId=1, tid=1 causes revert

preconditions: Ring buffer has wrapped at least once (numBlocks > blockRingBufferSize)
The parent blockHash of the current verification step matches the stale transition key at the reused slot
Hash collision probability: ~1/2^256

initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Cannot be intentionally triggered (requires hash collision), but code fails ungracefully when it should return 0

Test that schedule+execute can happen atomically with getMinDelay()==0
