threats.title: AddressManager can brick critical protocol dependencies by setting named addresses to EOA/zero, causing hard reverts in core flows
threats.hypothesis_description: Core flows assume resolver returns valid contract addresses (bridge, tier_provider, taiko_token, signal_service, verifiers). AddressManager.setAddress allows owner to set any address, including zero or an EOA. This can cause downstream external calls to revert (e.g., calling getTier on an EOA), halting proposing/proving/verifying or misrouting ETH to bridge.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a configuration/ops integrity risk: a single misconfiguration (or malicious governance action) can stop the chain. Need to verify operational safeguards around address changes and whether contract-level validation exists elsewhere (not shown).
deployment_assumptions: AddressManager ownership is controlled by timelock/governance or an operator that can misconfigure
No additional validation layer prevents setting invalid addresses for critical names
threats.config_evidence: AddressManager.setAddress has onlyOwner and no code/contract validation; DeployOnL1.s.sol transfers AddressManager ownership to timelock.

proof_of_signal.pattern: function setAddress(...) external onlyOwner { __addresses[_chainId][_name] = _newAddress; } // no validation

preconditions: Attacker controls AddressManager owner (or can pass governance to do so), OR operator error occurs
Critical names are used by TaikoL1/Verifier/Hook flows via resolve(...) with allowZero depending on call site
Affected function is executed after misconfiguration

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Resolver is a single dependency injection point; no typed/contract checks for critical targets.
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Any time an address is updated

operational_questions: Which resolved names are called with allowZero=false and will revert if unset?
Are there runbooks/tests before executing AddressManager changes (on staging forks)?
Is there monitoring for AddressSet events on critical names?
validation_requirements: Enumerate all resolve(name, allowZero) call sites and classify criticality
Simulate setting each critical name to EOA/zero and observe which core functions brick
Review timelock/governance procedures for address updates and emergency rollback

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Obtain AddressManager owner capability (directly or via timelock execution)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Set a critical dependency name (e.g., tier_provider, bridge, taiko_token) to an invalid address
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Trigger normal user workflows to observe reverts and liveness failure
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Optionally combine with upgrades to conceal or persist damage
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: High off-chain (privilege), low on-chain once owner

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: High: can halt deposits, proposals, proofs, verification, or state syncing
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Medium: requires correcting address mapping via owner/governance; may be delayed by timelock/pause states

investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Multiple address changes in short time window

threats.title: UUPS upgrade surface allows immediate logic replacement of core contracts if owner/timelock can execute upgradeTo
threats.hypothesis_description: EssentialContract inherits UUPSUpgradeable and authorizes upgrades via onlyOwner. If the owner is the timelock (as in deployment) and timelock delay can be bypassed (admin role) or governance is captured, an attacker can deploy a malicious implementation and upgrade critical proxies (TaikoL1, token, verifiers, address managers), enabling arbitrary state changes and asset theft/consensus corruption.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is an operational control-plane risk: upgradeability is intended, but combined with timelock bypass or weak governance it becomes a rapid takeover. Validate role assignments and whether upgrades are constrained by additional off-chain controls.
deployment_assumptions: Proxies are deployed for core contracts and owned by timelock/owner as per scripts
Upgrades are executed through timelock schedule/execute
TIMELOCK_ADMIN_ROLE (or equivalent) can reduce effective delay
threats.config_evidence: DeployOnL1.s.sol uses UpgradeScript scheduling upgradeTo(address) via timelock; EssentialContract._authorizeUpgrade is onlyOwner.

proof_of_signal.pattern: function _authorizeUpgrade(address) internal virtual override onlyOwner { }

preconditions: Attacker can cause owner-authorized upgrades (owner compromised / governance capture / timelock admin bypass)
Target contract is behind a UUPS proxy and upgradeTo is callable via proxy
Malicious implementation passes UUPS proxiableUUID checks

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: All core components share the same upgrade authority model; compromise cascades across protocol.

operational_questions: Which contracts are deployed behind UUPS proxies in production and who is owner?
Are there policy controls (e.g., multiple independent approvals) beyond on-chain roles?
Are upgrades monitored with canary checks and emergency rollback procedures?
validation_requirements: Enumerate proxy admin/owner for each core contract on deployment
Review timelock role assignments and whether admin bypass exists in practice
Simulate an upgrade and verify what state can be modified post-upgrade (e.g., sweeping TKO bonds)

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Obtain ability to execute owner-only calls on the proxy (via timelock/governance)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Deploy malicious implementation that preserves storage layout but adds backdoors
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Call upgradeTo on proxy via timelock execution
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Use backdoor functions to drain assets or corrupt verification/state roots

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Catastrophic: consensus corruption, bridge compromise, asset theft
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: All protocol operations controlled by upgraded contracts

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: upgradeTo/upgradeToAndCall calls on critical proxies; implementation address changes
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Unexpected new external methods or event patterns post-upgrade

threats.title: TaikoToken owner burn() enables targeted bond-holder griefing by destroying prover/contester balances mid-operation
threats.hypothesis_description: TaikoToken.burn allows onlyOwner to burn tokens from arbitrary addresses. If governance/owner abuses this (or is compromised), it can burn provers’/contesters’ TKO balances, preventing them from participating, contesting, or posting bonds, and potentially disrupting liveness/security of proving. This can be used selectively against competitors or defenders.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is an operationally realistic abuse vector under a compromised or malicious owner, with concrete on-chain action and direct impact on proving ecosystem. Validate ownership (timelock) and whether such power is acceptable/mitigated by policy.
deployment_assumptions: TaikoToken owner is a governance-controlled entity that could be compromised or misused
No social/operational constraints prevent arbitrary burns
threats.config_evidence: DeployOnL1.s.sol sets TaikoToken owner to timelock (TaikoToken.init owner param = timelock).

proof_of_signal.pattern: function burn(address _from, uint256 _amount) public onlyOwner { _burn(_from, _amount); }

preconditions: Owner/timelock can execute TaikoToken.burn
Target provers/contesters hold TKO in their own wallets (not fully locked elsewhere)
Protocol relies on those parties for proving/contesting capacity

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Arbitrary burn is a strong administrative primitive that can directly affect security participation.

operational_questions: Is burn intended only for slashing/compliance, and what policies restrict it?
Could burning key provers create a liveness collapse or reduce contestation capacity?
Is there transparency/alerting and community veto mechanisms?
validation_requirements: Confirm token owner on deployment and timelock constraints
Model impact of burning top prover balances on proving throughput and security
Review governance processes for burn proposals and emergency procedures

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Obtain timelock/governance execution capability for token owner actions
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Identify critical provers/contesters by on-chain activity
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Execute burn(_from, amount) to eliminate their ability to post bonds
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Exploit reduced security participation (e.g., fewer contesters) or stall proving

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Medium to High depending on targeted participants

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Burn events and sudden balance drops for active provers
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Decrease in prove/contest transactions due to capital removal
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Burns correlated with contentious disputes or competitions
